Entry-level motherboards will have the bare minimum of USB ports, SATA ports, audio/video ports, and Ethernet ports. Mid-range and high-end boards feature more and higher-specced ports — USB 3.2 Gen 2x2, USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-C, 2.5Gb Ethernet, Thunderbolt 4, and more.

Some motherboards also feature Wi-FI capabilities, with Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 6E being common on many mid-range motherboards. Similarly, Bluetooth connectivity is also helpful if you don't want to buy a separate Bluetooth dongle.

VRM design

Crucial for overclockers

The VRM of a motherboard is responsible for routing the right voltage to your PC components. It's made up of chokes, capacitors, and MOSFETs and determines the stability and quality of power delivered to your CPU and other components. While every motherboard has a VRM solution, only upper mid-range and high-end motherboards feature a VRM design capable of sustaining CPU overclocking and running high-end CPUs and GPUs.

A motherboard VRM is usually denoted by its number of phases (4, 8, 12, 16, 18, etc.) — the higher this number, the better the VRM design (generally). A lot of other things go into stable power delivery — the type of capacitors and chokes on the VRM, the cooling solution for the VRM, and the thickness of the PCB. If you're installing, say, Core i9 or Ryzen 9 CPUs on your motherboard, or you want a dependable motherboard for overclocking any CPU, it's always better to go with one with a high-quality VRM design.

Additional features

Nice-to-haves

There are some additional motherboard features that, while not important, can improve your overall experience. This could be onboard debug LEDs that can display error codes when something goes wrong with the motherboard, thus saving you hours while determining the underlying cause. Or it could also be a BIOS flashback button that doesn't need a CPU installed to update the BIOS.

Other quality-of-life features that you can look for on a motherboard are:

  • Sufficient fan and ARGB headers to accommodate all your components without needing additional hubs or controllers
  • Tool-less M.2 slots for easy SSD installation
  • Pre-installed M.2 heatsinks for high-speed SSDs
  • Integrated I/O shield so you don't forget to install it
  • RGB lighting for some extra bling
Related
5 features to look for in a new motherboard
Planning to buy a new motherboard? Here are some quality-of-life features you should keep an eye out for

Your motherboard is just as important as your CPU and GPU

Choosing a motherboard for a new PC should be a careful process, and you need to keep in mind both your current and future needs. The choice you make helps determine the longevity, expansion limits, connectivity options, overclocking headroom, and overall aesthetics of your device. Changing a motherboard isn't an easy affair, and isn't something you do often, so take the right amount of time before making the final decision.
item.content:encoded:
When building a PC, most of us spend a lot of time focusing on the CPU specs and GPU specs, but gloss over the key motherboard specs, as long as it's compatible with the processor. While matching your CPU and motherboard is obviously essential, there's a lot more that should go into a motherboard purchase decision.

To pick a motherboard that ticks every box for your specific requirements, you need to spend some time understanding the most important motherboard specifications. Combine that with your budget and you'll be that much closer to making the right choice.

Related
Why a motherboard might be the most important part to future-proof in your PC
Stop ignoring your motherboard.

Socket and chipset

First things first

The socket of your motherboard is the physical slot that determines which CPU you can install on it. Every processor is tied to a specific motherboard slot. For instance, Intel's 12th, 13th, and 14th Gen desktop processors are compatible only with Intel's LGA 1700 socket. Similarly, AMD's Ryzen 7000 CPUs are compatible only with AMD's AM5 socket. You can't install a CPU on a socket it wasn't created for.

While the socket is the most fundamental motherboard identifier, users generally search for motherboards using the chipset type. The chipset is responsible for linking all the components of a computer together and determining the speed of data transfer and the maximum number of ports and connectors on your motherboard. Every generation of Intel and AMD sockets is accompanied by a number of chipsets designed by the CPU manufacturers and produced by third-party motherboard manufacturers.

The currently available chipsets for the LGA 1700 and AM5 sockets are:

LGA 1700

  • H610: Entry-level chipset with two RAM slots and no PCIe 4.0
  • B660 and B760: Mainstream chipsets with up to four RAM slots, RAM overclocking, and PCIe 4.0 & PCIe 5.0 respectively
  • H670 and H770: Similar to B-series chipsets but support 128GB RAM
  • Z690 and Z790: Highest-end chipsets with CPU overclocking and the most PCIe lanes and ports

AM5

  • A620 and A620A: Entry-level chipsets with up to PCIe 5.0 NVMe support and RAM overclocking
  • B650 and B650E: Mainstream chipsets with more USB ports, CPU overclocking, PCIe 5.0 NVMe, and PCIe 4.0 graphics
  • X670 and X670E: Highest-end chipsets with the most PCIe lanes and ports, PCIe 5.0 NVMe, and PCIe 5.0 graphics

Also, some motherboard manufacturers provide unofficial features on entry-level motherboards; features that are usually not sanctioned by Intel or AMD on particular chipsets.

Related
5 things you will not get by spending more on a high-end motherboard
High-end motherboards can do a lot for your build. But these 5 things are not some of them

Form factor

Size does matter

The next crucial specification you need to know is the form factor of the motherboard you're buying. Desktop motherboards usually come in three popular sizes: ATX, micro-ATX, and mini-ITX. There are two major factors that should determine your choice of form factor — the size of your case and the number of components you want to install. Installing a smaller motherboard in a bigger case will look odd and unappealing, and you'll be limited by the expansion slots and ports on a smaller motherboard.

ATX motherboards are the largest of the three options and allow for additional memory slots, storage connectors, PCIe slots, fan headers, and a better Voltage Regulator Module (VRM) design. They're also usually better for cooling than the other two sizes. micro-ATX or mATX boards are scaled-down ATX boards with most of the same functionality but slightly fewer ports and a generally weaker VRM design. Finally, mini-ITX motherboards are ideal for small-form-factor builds, and feature only the most essential ports and connectors.

Related
How to pick a PC case: 5 things to consider while buying a chassis for your build
Here's how to pick a PC case for your next build.

RAM slots – type, number, and speed

DDR4 or DDR5 — that is the question

The type of memory you can use also depends on your motherboard — a motherboard will only support one type of memory. Today, DDR4 and DDR5 are the most common memory types on the market, with DDR5 being the latest standard. If you're buying an Intel motherboard, you'll have the option of choosing between DDR4 and DDR5 versions of the same motherboard, as Intel CPUs support both memory types. But, AMD's latest motherboards with the AM5 socket support only DDR5 RAM, with DDR4 support available only on the last-gen AM4 motherboards.

Building a DDR4-based system can save you money due to the cheaper DDR4 motherboards and last-gen processors.

You also need to take into account the number of RAM slots on your motherboard. Two slots can prove to be a limitation if you want to be able to expand your memory in the future, whereas four slots give you that additional freedom. Lastly, the maximum RAM speed differs from motherboard to motherboard. For instance, higher-end motherboards might support memory kits rated to 8,000MT/s, but budget motherboards might limit that to 6,400MT/s.

Building a DDR4-based system can save you money due to the cheaper DDR4 motherboards and last-gen processors, but you'll lose out on some features, and you will be investing in a system that can't be upgraded. A DDR5-based build, on the other hand, will allow you to seamlessly drop in a next-gen CPU (in the case of AMD) on the same motherboard, saving the cost and hassle of buying a new motherboard.

Related
Is DDR4 still viable in 2023?
Many believe the advent of DDR5 has already killed off the significance of DDR4. Let’s see if that’s really the case.

Storage connectors — type, number, and speed

Which SSD do you want to use?

The next important aspect of a motherboard is the generation of PCIe support it has. While this might not matter much for graphics cards that still work great on PCIe 3.0 slots, it matters for the kind of storage you want to install. PCIe 3.0 NVMe SSDs are mostly fine for gaming-only builds, as they allow you to leverage much faster loading times in games compared to conventional HDDs, and are sufficiently fast for booting your operating system as well.

But, PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSDs are the sweet spot for most people due to their significantly higher read/write speeds and better affordability compared to the newer Gen5 drives. Coming to the PCIe 5.0 SSDs, these are worthwhile only for users who can take advantage of their blistering sequential read/write speeds, which are useful when copying lots of files, or using productivity applications.

Do note the number of PCIe lanes available on each of the M.2 slots, as not all slots on a motherboard enjoy the same maximum speed.

Having the desired PCIe support — Gen4 or Gen5 — on your motherboard can be a make-or-break factor. Moreover, the number of M.2 slots on your motherboard will determine how many SSDs you can install. Do note the number of PCIe lanes available on each of the M.2 slots, as not all slots on a motherboard enjoy the same maximum speed.

Related
It's 2024 and PCIe 5.0 SSDs are still not worth it for gaming
PCIe 5.0 SSDs hold the key to transformative gaming experiences. If only those promises weren't years away from actually panning out.

Expansion slots — type, number, and speed

For the enthusiasts

PCIe slots on your motherboard are needed for graphics cards, sound cards, network cards, and other components that require a high-speed connection directly to the CPU. While sound cards and network cards might not be that common today, you probably have a discrete graphics card you want to install on your motherboard. This makes the type of PCIe slots on your motherboard important.

Graphics cards today work just fine on PCIe 3.0 slots, but those who want a more future-proof motherboard will value PCIe 4.0 or PCIe 5.0 slots. Plus, you also want to focus on the number of PCIe slots — in case of multiple graphics cards —and the number and configuration of PCIe lanes available on your motherboard. This determines how many high-speed components you can run simultaneously at full speed, like graphics cards, SSDs, and other expansion cards.

Related
PCI Express 5 (PCIe 5.0): Here's everything you need to know about the new standard
PCIe 5.0 is the next big thing in the computing space.

Connectivity ports

How many peripherals do you have?

What probably escapes the attention of many users is the number of I/O ports on the motherboard. This determines the number and performance of your peripheral devices. Entry-level motherboards will have the bare minimum of USB ports, SATA ports, audio/video ports, and Ethernet ports. Mid-range and high-end boards feature more and higher-specced ports — USB 3.2 Gen 2x2, USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-C, 2.5Gb Ethernet, Thunderbolt 4, and more.

Some motherboards also feature Wi-FI capabilities, with Wi-Fi 6 or Wi-Fi 6E being common on many mid-range motherboards. Similarly, Bluetooth connectivity is also helpful if you don't want to buy a separate Bluetooth dongle.

VRM design

Crucial for overclockers

The VRM of a motherboard is responsible for routing the right voltage to your PC components. It's made up of chokes, capacitors, and MOSFETs and determines the stability and quality of power delivered to your CPU and other components. While every motherboard has a VRM solution, only upper mid-range and high-end motherboards feature a VRM design capable of sustaining CPU overclocking and running high-end CPUs and GPUs.

A motherboard VRM is usually denoted by its number of phases (4, 8, 12, 16, 18, etc.) — the higher this number, the better the VRM design (generally). A lot of other things go into stable power delivery — the type of capacitors and chokes on the VRM, the cooling solution for the VRM, and the thickness of the PCB. If you're installing, say, Core i9 or Ryzen 9 CPUs on your motherboard, or you want a dependable motherboard for overclocking any CPU, it's always better to go with one with a high-quality VRM design.

Additional features

Nice-to-haves

There are some additional motherboard features that, while not important, can improve your overall experience. This could be onboard debug LEDs that can display error codes when something goes wrong with the motherboard, thus saving you hours while determining the underlying cause. Or it could also be a BIOS flashback button that doesn't need a CPU installed to update the BIOS.

Other quality-of-life features that you can look for on a motherboard are:

  • Sufficient fan and ARGB headers to accommodate all your components without needing additional hubs or controllers
  • Tool-less M.2 slots for easy SSD installation
  • Pre-installed M.2 heatsinks for high-speed SSDs
  • Integrated I/O shield so you don't forget to install it
  • RGB lighting for some extra bling
Related
5 features to look for in a new motherboard
Planning to buy a new motherboard? Here are some quality-of-life features you should keep an eye out for

Your motherboard is just as important as your CPU and GPU

Choosing a motherboard for a new PC should be a careful process, and you need to keep in mind both your current and future needs. The choice you make helps determine the longevity, expansion limits, connectivity options, overclocking headroom, and overall aesthetics of your device. Changing a motherboard isn't an easy affair, and isn't something you do often, so take the right amount of time before making the final decision.

item.title: Transform that boring outlet with this compact extender from Anker that's now nearly 30% off for a limited time
item.itunes:title: Transform that boring outlet with this compact extender from Anker that's now nearly 30% off for a limited time
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Anker Portable Outlet Extender with Foldable Plug
$22 $30 Save $8

Take your boring outlets to another level with this Anker outlet extender that's perfect for the home or office. It's also ultra-compact, which makes it perfect for travel too. Right now, you can score 27% off this device for a limited time.

$22 at Amazon

If you're looking for a compact charging solution that's going to be able to take a boring AC outlet, and elevate its look and functionality up a few notches, then this Anker Portable Outlet Extender is going to be for you. Not only does the outlet extender look better than those basic power strips that we all probably have lying around, but it also provides some excellent functionality with its two outlets and USB port selection.

Now, normally, this outlet comes priced at $29.99, which is quite pricey. But you can now score a decent discount that knocks 27% off, driving the price down to one of its lowest this year. And since this is a limited-time deal that could stop at any minute, you're going to want to grab it right now while you still have the chance. Of course, since this is coming from Amazon, you'll get quick shipping if you're a Prime member and easy returns.

What's great about this Anker Portable Outlet Extender?

Source: Anker

Anker gets a lot of things right with this outlet extender, producing a compact solution that's not only ultra-portable but highly functional as well. To give you an idea, the dimensions come in roughly the size of a credit card, but with an inch of thickness, which is pretty small. When it comes to port selection, you're going to get two USB-A and one USB-C port, with a maximum charging capacity of 30W.

That means you're going to easily be able to charge smartphones and even tablets too. While some laptops might even be able to work off this unit, the charging speed might be a bit slow, but if that's okay, then this could become your ultimate all-in-one travel charger when you're away from your home. Of course, you'll also get Anker's ActiveShield 2.0 technology, which will ensure safe charging for you and your devices.

Now, if that doesn't provide some peace of mind, you're in for a treat, because Anker provides an 18-month warranty for this device, just in case there might be any issues with it. Furthermore, you'll also get Anker's $200,000 connected equipment warranty, just in case the device happens to cause damage to your connected products. Overall, this is just one of those devices that really offers some unique features, making it the perfect outlet extender to take with you on the road.

So be sure to grab it while you can because, at this price, you're getting a pretty good deal. And if this isn't quite what you're looking for, and you want some other charger options, be sure to check out our favorite GaN chargers.

item.title: With Windows 11 24H2 on the way and Windows 10 reaching End of Life, what can AI hold-outs do?
item.itunes:title: With Windows 11 24H2 on the way and Windows 10 reaching End of Life, what can AI hold-outs do?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Windows 11 has bloat, ads, and annoyances, but offers future potential with 24H2 update and AI features, like Copilot+.
  • Stay on Windows 10, switch to Linux, or stick with older Windows 11 version to avoid Windows 11 issues, ads, and UI gripes.
  • If you don't want an outdated OS or can't pay for ESUs, modding community help may be your only hope. Await updates to address AI concerns.

Microsoft has been annoying Windows users for quite a while now, and understandably so. Windows 11 is packed full of bloat, advertisements, and annoying quirks that people would rather not deal with. I say this as someone who actually generally enjoys Windows 11 as well, though it took me a while to actually use it on my daily PC. Until recently, I was a Windows 10 hold-out, but anti-cheat changes with esports titles that I play essentially forced me to upgrade.

With that, though, it got me thinking. What are the options for people who want to stay back? Windows 11 isn't just a product of what it is now, but what it's going to be. Windows 11 24H2 is around the corner, and while the Copilot+ features are only for Snapdragon X series laptops for now, there's nothing that actually stops them working on Nvidia or AMD GPUs in the future. When that time comes, what can the holdouts do?

Related
Windows 11 2024 Update: What's new and why it's not Windows 12
The Windows 11 2024 Update will bring a ton of changes to the operating system, including better support for Arm devices.

Dread it, run from it, AI still arrives

Windows will have AI whether you like it or not

The sad truth is that AI will still play a part in Windows whether you like it or not, and I expect that the Copilot+ features will eventually make their way to high-end PCs. When that day comes, I also fully expect the Windows modding community to find a way to disable all of it very quickly, and then it might turn into a cat and mouse game with Microsoft.

That's likely the best case scenario, though. Nobody wants to have to mod their Windows just to make it the way they like it, especially when for most, Windows 10 is a perfectly fine operating system. Windows 10 is end of life next year though, and at that stage, it starts becoming an insecure operating system. It may not be immediately susceptible to the entire world in terms of hacking, but it's unquestionably made significantly more insecure as a result.

On the flip side, early reports of Microsoft's Recall don't particularly lend faith to it being particularly secure, either. Kevin Beaumont demonstrated how you can exfiltrate data from Copilot+'s Recall extremely easily, as the database storing information is kept on-device and easily accessible at all times. There is no secure element to it at present, though I would hope that Microsoft updates it to make it so in the future.

Switch to Linux, or stay on Windows 10

Or, I guess, stay on an older Windows 11 version with its own problems

Another option AI holdouts have who are getting sick of Windows is to switch to Linux. You can get a lot more control over your operating system and the software running on it, and it's expected that you would. In contrast, Windows isn't meant to be modified, especially in the way that people essentially hack it to remove certain functionality.

The other option is to stay on Windows 10 through its End of Life period, which you can pay for a three-year extension of security updates (called Extended Security Updates, or ESUs) in order to do that. It's expensive though, setting you back $61 for the first year, $122 for the second year, and $244 for the third year. That's a lot of money to be on an outdated operating system.

The third option you have is to stay on an older version of Windows 11, but then you still have the downsides of Windows 11 like advertisements, bloat, and the UI (which many people hate). If that's the case, then you're really stuck for choice here. That's without even getting into your hardware that may not even support Windows 11, which is yet another difficulty users may face in trying to upgrade, if they eventually decide to take the plunge.

Related
Windows 10 will reach end of life next year, but what does that mean?
Windows 10 will no longer be supported by Microsoft

What can you do?

To be honest, not much

If you're not comfortable with staying on an outdated operating system, hate Windows 11, but also can't (or don't want to) pay for ESUs, then you're out of luck. Your only real option is to take the plunge and hope to be bailed out by the modding community. Those AI features can definitely be disabled with a bit of work, but what that work entails and how it survives Windows updates is obviously unknown at this point.

There are plenty of Windows fans out there that also like these new features too, so it's not as if they're going anywhere. Plus, Microsoft can easily sell them as additional extras that you won't get anywhere else; which is true to an extent. Apple's WWDC is just around the corner and may have something to say about that, but as it stands, Microsoft is in a league of its own.

We'll be waiting to see how the community adapts to Windows 10's End of Life at the end of next year, and with Windows 11 24H2 coming out later this year, maybe we'll see some novel ways of dealing with the AI built-in to it that makes users more comfortable upgrading.

If you're not comfortable with staying on an outdated operating system, hate Windows 11, but also can't (or don't want to) pay for ESUs, then you're out of luck. Your only real option is to take the plunge and hope to be bailed out by the modding community. Those AI features can definitely be disabled with a bit of work, but what that work entails and how it survives Windows updates is obviously unknown at this point.

There are plenty of Windows fans out there that also like these new features too, so it's not as if they're going anywhere. Plus, Microsoft can easily sell them as additional extras that you won't get anywhere else; which is true to an extent. Apple's WWDC is just around the corner and may have something to say about that, but as it stands, Microsoft is in a league of its own.

We'll be waiting to see how the community adapts to Windows 10's End of Life at the end of next year, and with Windows 11 24H2 coming out later this year, maybe we'll see some novel ways of dealing with the AI built-in to it that makes users more comfortable upgrading.
item.content:encoded:
Key Takeaways

  • Windows 11 has bloat, ads, and annoyances, but offers future potential with 24H2 update and AI features, like Copilot+.
  • Stay on Windows 10, switch to Linux, or stick with older Windows 11 version to avoid Windows 11 issues, ads, and UI gripes.
  • If you don't want an outdated OS or can't pay for ESUs, modding community help may be your only hope. Await updates to address AI concerns.

Microsoft has been annoying Windows users for quite a while now, and understandably so. Windows 11 is packed full of bloat, advertisements, and annoying quirks that people would rather not deal with. I say this as someone who actually generally enjoys Windows 11 as well, though it took me a while to actually use it on my daily PC. Until recently, I was a Windows 10 hold-out, but anti-cheat changes with esports titles that I play essentially forced me to upgrade.

With that, though, it got me thinking. What are the options for people who want to stay back? Windows 11 isn't just a product of what it is now, but what it's going to be. Windows 11 24H2 is around the corner, and while the Copilot+ features are only for Snapdragon X series laptops for now, there's nothing that actually stops them working on Nvidia or AMD GPUs in the future. When that time comes, what can the holdouts do?

Related
Windows 11 2024 Update: What's new and why it's not Windows 12
The Windows 11 2024 Update will bring a ton of changes to the operating system, including better support for Arm devices.

Dread it, run from it, AI still arrives

Windows will have AI whether you like it or not

The sad truth is that AI will still play a part in Windows whether you like it or not, and I expect that the Copilot+ features will eventually make their way to high-end PCs. When that day comes, I also fully expect the Windows modding community to find a way to disable all of it very quickly, and then it might turn into a cat and mouse game with Microsoft.

That's likely the best case scenario, though. Nobody wants to have to mod their Windows just to make it the way they like it, especially when for most, Windows 10 is a perfectly fine operating system. Windows 10 is end of life next year though, and at that stage, it starts becoming an insecure operating system. It may not be immediately susceptible to the entire world in terms of hacking, but it's unquestionably made significantly more insecure as a result.

On the flip side, early reports of Microsoft's Recall don't particularly lend faith to it being particularly secure, either. Kevin Beaumont demonstrated how you can exfiltrate data from Copilot+'s Recall extremely easily, as the database storing information is kept on-device and easily accessible at all times. There is no secure element to it at present, though I would hope that Microsoft updates it to make it so in the future.

Switch to Linux, or stay on Windows 10

Or, I guess, stay on an older Windows 11 version with its own problems

Another option AI holdouts have who are getting sick of Windows is to switch to Linux. You can get a lot more control over your operating system and the software running on it, and it's expected that you would. In contrast, Windows isn't meant to be modified, especially in the way that people essentially hack it to remove certain functionality.

The other option is to stay on Windows 10 through its End of Life period, which you can pay for a three-year extension of security updates (called Extended Security Updates, or ESUs) in order to do that. It's expensive though, setting you back $61 for the first year, $122 for the second year, and $244 for the third year. That's a lot of money to be on an outdated operating system.

The third option you have is to stay on an older version of Windows 11, but then you still have the downsides of Windows 11 like advertisements, bloat, and the UI (which many people hate). If that's the case, then you're really stuck for choice here. That's without even getting into your hardware that may not even support Windows 11, which is yet another difficulty users may face in trying to upgrade, if they eventually decide to take the plunge.

Related
Windows 10 will reach end of life next year, but what does that mean?
Windows 10 will no longer be supported by Microsoft

What can you do?

To be honest, not much

If you're not comfortable with staying on an outdated operating system, hate Windows 11, but also can't (or don't want to) pay for ESUs, then you're out of luck. Your only real option is to take the plunge and hope to be bailed out by the modding community. Those AI features can definitely be disabled with a bit of work, but what that work entails and how it survives Windows updates is obviously unknown at this point.

There are plenty of Windows fans out there that also like these new features too, so it's not as if they're going anywhere. Plus, Microsoft can easily sell them as additional extras that you won't get anywhere else; which is true to an extent. Apple's WWDC is just around the corner and may have something to say about that, but as it stands, Microsoft is in a league of its own.

We'll be waiting to see how the community adapts to Windows 10's End of Life at the end of next year, and with Windows 11 24H2 coming out later this year, maybe we'll see some novel ways of dealing with the AI built-in to it that makes users more comfortable upgrading.
